one_line_takeaway: QuantileMark replaces vocabulary-partition channels with equal-mass CDF-interval partitioning to give every multi-bit watermark symbol an identical probability budget, provably eliminating message-dependent quality/detection asymmetry and empirically beating MPAC and StealthInk on bit accuracy, AUC, and perplexity preservation on C4 and LFQA -- but like its baselines it degrades sharply under paraphrasing and requires white-box, provider-internal detection.
key_finding: On Table 1 (24 bits embedded in 300 tokens): on C4, QuantileMark achieves Bit Acc 0.9893, AUC 0.9995, TPR@1%FPR 0.9840, PPL 7.404 (vs. no-watermark PPL 7.684), detection time 0.343s; MPAC achieves Bit Acc 0.9702, AUC 0.9887, TPR@1%FPR 0.9800, PPL 10.351, time 0.103s; StealthInk achieves Bit Acc 0.9003, AUC 0.9869, TPR@1%FPR 0.7920, PPL 8.235, time 0.645s. On LFQA, QuantileMark achieves Bit Acc 0.9500, AUC 0.9997, TPR@1%FPR 1.0, PPL 2.759 (vs. no-watermark PPL 2.647); MPAC achieves Bit Acc 0.8770, AUC 0.9756, TPR@1%FPR 0.8056, PPL 3.793; StealthInk achieves Bit Acc 0.7447, AUC 0.8301, TPR@1%FPR 0.2425, PPL 2.636. Table 2 (C4 robustness, 24 bits/300 tokens): under no attack QuantileMark Bit Acc 0.9893/AUC 0.9995; under copy-paste (eps=0.2) 0.9730/0.9972; synonym substitution (eps=0.2) 0.9712/0.9963; random deletion (eps=0.1) 0.8712/0.9426; DIPPER paraphrase 0.7640/0.7609 -- versus MPAC's paraphrase result of 0.7201/0.7936 and StealthInk's 0.6829/0.6536, i.e. all methods degrade sharply under paraphrasing, and the paper explicitly states 'robust detection under paraphrasing remains an open challenge across all existing multi-bit watermarking schemes.' The m-ablation (Figure 4, LFQA) shows moderate symbol sizes m=2,3 perform best, while m=4 sharply reduces bit accuracy despite detection AUC remaining strong, attributed to the information-capacity bound in Appendix A.3. Table 3 shows downstream utility (BLEU/ROUGE-1/BERTScore for translation, ROUGE-1/BERTScore/PPL for summarization) for QuantileMark is nearly identical to the no-watermark baseline (e.g., BLEU 17.41 vs. 17.55 no-watermark vs. MPAC's 16.89), outperforming MPAC's degradation while matching or slightly beating StealthInk.
core_contribution: QuantileMark is a white-box, provider-internal multi-bit LLM watermarking scheme that embeds provenance messages by partitioning the cumulative probability interval [0,1) into M equal-mass quantile bins (rather than partitioning the vocabulary, as prior multi-bit watermarks do) and sampling strictly within the bin corresponding to the target message symbol. The paper's own novel contributions, stated explicitly in its introduction, are: (1) the QuantileMark embedding/detection framework itself, which guarantees a fixed 1/M probability budget per symbol regardless of context entropy via quantile-interval partitioning plus a white-box teacher-forced detector that computes posteriors over latent bins and aggregates Log Posterior Odds (LPO) evidence; (2) a formal definition and proof of 'message-unbiasedness' (Lemma 1, proved via linearity of expectation and the partition-of-unity property of quantile bins) establishing that averaging the watermarked distribution over messages recovers the base distribution; and (3) empirical demonstration that QuantileMark outperforms vocabulary-partition and RNG-space baselines on C4 continuation and LFQA long-form QA in multi-bit recovery and detection robustness while preserving generation quality.
threat_model: This is not an adversarial-attacker security paper in the classical sense (no adversary model against the watermark's cryptographic secrecy is analyzed); the setting is 'provider-internal' attribution/provenance: the LLM provider embeds a message (e.g., user ID, model version, timestamp) into generated text using a secret key K, and only the same provider (who controls both generation and detection, with white-box access to the model at detection time) can decode the message. The stated real-world purpose is misinformation mitigation, abuse response, copyright dispute resolution, and enterprise compliance. The paper does evaluate robustness to a specific adversarial/degradation threat: an entity attempting to erase or corrupt the embedded message via four post-hoc 'scrubbing attacks' on generated text -- copy-paste mixing with non-watermarked text (epsilon=0.2), synonym substitution (epsilon=0.2), random token deletion (epsilon=0.1), and DIPPER-based paraphrasing -- plus a 'detector-generator mismatch' scenario where the detector's decoding hyperparameters (temperature tau-hat, top-k-hat) differ from those used at generation time. No adversary model is given for key extraction, model theft, or spoofing another provider's watermark.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations (verbatim from its Limitations section): (1) robustness remains limited under heavy paraphrasing and other edits that substantially rewrite local contexts, since these disrupt position allocation and the seed stream used for per-step relabeling/evidence aggregation, and improving this would require position-allocation strategies tolerant of sequence misalignment; (2) the study assumes white-box access at detection time in a provider-internal setting, ruling out public/black-box verification from text alone in its current form, and the paper explicitly did not systematically study how model mismatch, a proxy/distilled model, or distribution shift affect reconstructed geometry and recovery, even though it speculates such transfer 'may' work; (3) experimental scope was limited to a small set of tasks (C4 continuation, LFQA) and model families (Llama-2-7B, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct), and broader decoding-policy settings were not evaluated. Additionally, the m=4 ablation shows the scheme's own information-theoretic bound (per-step evidence capped by token self-information) causes bit-accuracy collapse at higher symbol resolution under fixed message length, a structural tradeoff the authors acknowledge but do not solve.

datasets_used: C4 (Raffel et al., 2020) -- open-ended continuation task
ELI5 / LFQA (Fan et al., 2019) -- long-form QA task
CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015) -- downstream summarization utility eval
WMT'14 En-Ro (Bojar et al., 2014) -- downstream machine translation utility eval
quantitative_results: C4, 24-bit message/300 tokens: QuantileMark Bit Acc 0.9893, AUC 0.9995, TPR@1%FPR 0.9840, PPL 7.404, detection time 0.343s (no-watermark PPL 7.684)
LFQA, 24-bit message/300 tokens: QuantileMark Bit Acc 0.9500, AUC 0.9997, TPR@1%FPR 1.0, PPL 2.759, detection time 0.348s (no-watermark PPL 2.647)

C4 robustness (Bit Acc / AUC) for QuantileMark: no attack 0.9893/0.9995; copy-paste eps=0.2: 0.9730/0.9972; synonym eps=0.2: 0.9712/0.9963; deletion eps=0.1: 0.8712/0.9426; DIPPER paraphrase: 0.7640/0.7609
LFQA m-ablation: m=2 and m=3 give best bit accuracy at fixed 24-bit message length; m=4 causes a sharp bit-accuracy drop despite AUC remaining strong
Downstream MT (BLEU/ROUGE-1/BERTScore): no-watermark 17.55/49.13/35.82; QuantileMark 17.41/48.99/35.99; MPAC 16.89/47.98/34.89; StealthInk 17.42/48.97/35.75
Downstream summarization (ROUGE-1/BERTScore/PPL): no-watermark 37.42/21.40/5.18; QuantileMark 37.44/21.37/5.21; MPAC 36.81/20.57/5.81; StealthInk 37.08/20.83/5.72
baselines_compared: MPAC (Yoo et al., 2024) -- distortionary vocabulary-partition multi-bit watermark, m=2, gamma=0.25, delta=2.0
StealthInk (Jiang et al., 2025) -- distribution-preserving multi-bit watermark, m=1
No-watermark baseline (unwatermarked generations / human references) for PPL and utility comparison
